title: Few people in low-income countries own a smartphone
subtitle: Share of people who report that their main mobile phone is a smartphone
source: The Global Findex Database (2025), indicators: [con9a] “Main mobile phone is a smartphone (%, age 15+)”
visdescription: Vertical dot plot showing the share of people who report that their main mobile phone is a smartphone, by country and income group
mobile_not_main: Share of people not owning a smartphone
lack_of_money: Lack of money as a reason for having no smartphone
keyFactLabel: of people in low-income countries own a smartphone

scenes.text: The share of people who[emphasis: do not own a smartphone ]is high in poorer countries, reaching nearly[emphasis: ][emphasis: 80 percent] in [emphasis: low-income economies] and about[emphasis: 50 percent] in[emphasis: ][emphasis: lower-middle-income economies].

In several low-income countries—including [emphasis: Malawi][emphasis: , ][emphasis: Madagascar][emphasis: , and ][emphasis: Chad][emphasis: —less than 14 percent of adults own a smartphone]. This highlights the strong association between income levels and smartphone ownership, and points to the persistent barriers faced by the poorest in accessing more advanced mobile technologies.

_meta.approvalNote: hand layouts; coverage_map is ranked-bars proxy not Mapbox
